description: This figure contains two optical micrographs of high Mn-Al lightweight austenitic steels both etched with 10% nital. The first subgraph (acquired in bright-field mode, scale bar 100 μm) shows the microstructure of as-cast Fe-28Mn-3Al-0.9Si-1.2C-0.6Mo steel, which consists of large primary austenite dendrites with millimeter-scale grain size. The second subgraph (acquired in differential interference contrast mode, scale bar 50 μm) displays the hot-rolled Fe-29Mn-6Al-0.9C-0.29Si-0.44Nb-0.44Mo steel, composed of fine equiaxed austenite grains (average grain size 50 μm) with annealing twins, and grain boundaries are pinned by NbC precipitates. The comparison confirms that hot rolling and recrystallization can significantly refine the coarse dendritic microstructure of as-cast steels, supporting that thermomechanical processing optimizes the microstructure of this class of lightweight steels to achieve improved mechanical properties.

This figure presents two 530 °C age-hardening curves for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels, plotting Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) against aging time on a log scale ranging from 0.1 to 1000 hours. The first subplot assesses low-phosphorus (<0.003 wt% P) steels with three Si levels: 0.59 wt%, 1.07 wt%, and 1.56 wt%, showing that higher Si content increases hardness at equivalent aging time without significant change to peak hardness. The second subplot assesses steels with ~1 wt% Si and four P levels: 0.001 wt%, 0.006 wt%, 0.018 wt%, and 0.043 wt%, showing that higher P content substantially elevates both the age hardening rate and peak hardness. The data support the conclusion that both Si and P promote κ-carbide precipitation during aging, leading to increased hardness of the alloy system.

This figure shows two dark-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels aged at 490 °C for 63 h, with different silicon contents. The first image is for the 0.59 wt.% Si steel, the second for the 1.56 wt.% Si steel, both with a 10 nm scale bar. Inset selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns are provided for each sample to confirm the crystalline structure of the austenite matrix and κ-carbide precipitates. Visual comparison demonstrates that the 1.56 wt.% Si steel has obviously larger size and higher number density of nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates than the 0.59 wt.% Si steel. This observation verifies that higher silicon content accelerates κ-carbide precipitation, increases its volume fraction during aging, and contributes to the enhanced age hardening effect of the steel.

This figure presents two dark-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C-0.5Mo lightweight austenitic steels with different phosphorus contents, both aged at 570 °C for 0.5 h. The first image (corresponding to 0.001 wt.% P steel) has a 10 nm scale bar, while the second image (corresponding to 0.043 wt.% P steel) has a 25 nm scale bar. Selected area electron diffraction patterns are inset in the upper right of each image to confirm the presence of perovskite-structured κ-carbides. Comparison shows κ-carbides in low-P steel are very small (only a few unit cells in size) with high number density, while κ-carbides in high-P steel have a larger average size of ~5 nm, higher number density, and preferentially coarsen along <100> crystallographic directions. This figure confirms phosphorus significantly accelerates κ-carbide precipitation and spinodal decomposition during aging, supporting the mechanism of phosphorus-induced embrittlement in this high Mn-Al steel system.

This figure presents two carbon concentration maps obtained from atom probe tomography (APT) analysis, demonstrating spinodal decomposition behavior in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels aged at 530 °C for 10 h. The left map corresponds to the steel with 0.006 wt.% phosphorus, while the right map corresponds to the steel with 0.043 wt.% phosphorus. Both maps use a color scale ranging from blue (2 at.% carbon) to red (6 at.% carbon), with x and y axes spanning from -30 nm to 30 nm. The high-phosphorus steel exhibits significantly larger, more prominent carbon-enriched regions compared to the low-phosphorus steel where carbon concentration fluctuations are smaller and less distinct. These results confirm that higher phosphorus content greatly accelerates the spinodal decomposition rate during aging, supporting the core conclusion that phosphorus promotes κ-carbide precipitation kinetics, which leads to reduced impact toughness and dynamic fracture toughness in age-hardened high manganese aluminum steels.

This optical micrograph displays the microstructure of hot-rolled Fe-30Mn-9Al-1.01Si-0.9C-0.5Mo lightweight austenitic steel in the peak aged condition (aged 60 h at 530 °C), imaged using differential interference contrast after nital etching, with a 20 μm scale bar provided. Fine equiaxed austenite grains (average grain size 10–20 μm) with visible annealing twins are observed, alongside preferentially etched grain boundaries. The caption states that the grain boundary etching arises from κ-carbide precipitation at grain boundaries during peak aging, while banding features come from alloy segregation during processing. This microstructure evidence supports the core conclusion that peak aging induced grain boundary κ-carbide precipitation reduces the alloy's elongation from 54% (solution treated state) to 14% and lowers its fracture toughness, as grain boundary carbides act as crack initiation sites during mechanical loading.

This figure consists of two scatter plots comparing the mechanical properties of wrought and cast Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels. The first subplot plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, in MPa) against aging time (in hours, log scale from 0.1 to 100 h, where 0.1 h corresponds to the solution-treated condition): blue squares represent wrought samples (grain refined to 10-20 μm via 84% thickness reduction hot rolling at 900 °C followed by solution treatment), red circles represent cast samples. UTS of wrought samples increases from ~950 MPa to ~1180 MPa with extended aging time, and is consistently higher than cast samples at equivalent aging durations. The second subplot plots UTS against percent elongation to failure: wrought samples exhibit higher elongation than cast samples at similar UTS levels, demonstrating better strength-ductility synergy from grain refinement. The figure supports that grain refinement via thermomechanical processing improves both aging hardening efficiency and tensile property matching of this high Mn-Al steel.

This set of scanning electron microscopy images presents fracture surfaces of solution-treated and quenched Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C lightweight austenitic steel Charpy V-notch specimens with different phosphorus contents. The first sub-figure with a 20 μm scale bar corresponds to the 0.001 wt.% P sample, showing dense dimpled morphology characteristic of ductile tearing fracture. The second sub-figure (20 μm scale bar) and third sub-figure (100 μm scale bar) correspond to the 0.07 wt.% P sample, exhibiting low-energy cleavage-like fracture with dendritic patterns formed by fracture along {001} crystallographic planes. The clear fracture mode transition from ductile to brittle with rising P content directly verifies phosphorus-induced embrittlement in this alloy system, supporting the conclusion that phosphorus short-range ordering reduces the cleavage strength of {001} planes, leading to decreased notch toughness of the steel.

This chart presents room-temperature Charpy V-notch impact energy (left y-axis, unit J/cm², black circular data points) and Brinell Hardness Number (right y-axis, grey square data points) as a function of phosphorus content (x-axis, in wt.%) for age-hardened Fe-30Mn-9Al-1Si-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels, with samples aged at 530 °C for 10 h. Error bars indicate the variability of measured values for each composition. As phosphorus content increases from ~0 wt.% to ~0.045 wt.%, impact energy drops drastically from ~137 J/cm² to ~24 J/cm², while Brinell hardness increases from ~292 to ~342. This figure supports the conclusion that phosphorus significantly enhances age hardening but causes severe embrittlement in this high Mn-Al steel system, so strict control of phosphorus content is required to retain acceptable notch toughness for application.

This figure includes two subplots investigating inclusions and their impact on mechanical performance of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steel. Subplot (a) is a bar chart presenting the percentage distribution of different inclusions in a 0.001 wt% P alloy after calcium treatment and argon stirring: AlN is the most dominant inclusion at ~26%, followed by pores (~17%), Mn-AlO (~14%), AlN-MnO (~12%), and other minor inclusions such as MnO, MnS, oxides, nitrides, and oxysulfides. Subplot (b) is a scatter plot showing the correlation between AlN density (unit: particles/mm²) and Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy (unit: Joules) for 5 experimental heats (base, Ca-treated, Ca+Ce treated, Ca+Ce+Ar stirred, Ca+Ce+Ar teapot treated) plus industrial heat samples. A clear negative trend is observed: higher AlN density directly correlates to lower CVN impact toughness. The figure supports the core conclusion that AlN inclusions are detrimental to the impact toughness of the studied high Mn-Al steel, and melt practices such as argon stirring can effectively reduce AlN content to improve mechanical performance.

This group of four plots present Charpy V-notch impact energy (unit: Joules) as a function of testing temperature (ranging from -50 °C to 30 °C, unit: Celsius) for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels with two Si contents and two aging conditions. The four subplots correspond to 1.07 wt.% Si aged at 530 °C for 12 h, 1.56 wt.% Si aged at 530 °C for 12 h, 1.07 wt.% Si aged at 530 °C for 30 h, and 1.56 wt.% Si aged at 530 °C for 30 h respectively. All samples show an increasing trend of impact energy with rising testing temperature, exhibiting clear ductile-to-brittle transition behavior. Under the same aging condition, higher Si content leads to lower impact energy across all test temperatures: room temperature impact energy decreases from 92 J (1.07% Si, 12 h aged) to 68 J (1.56% Si, 12 h aged), and from 58 J (1.07% Si, 30 h aged) to 35 J (1.56% Si, 30 h aged). Longer aging time also reduces impact energy for the same Si content, attributed to increased κ-carbide precipitation. The figure supports that Si addition and extended aging time deteriorate the impact toughness of the studied high Mn-Al steel.

This group includes two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fracture surfaces, both equipped with a 20 μm scale bar. The first image presents the fracture surface of a Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C austenitic lightweight steel specimen with 1.07 wt.% Si, which was aged at 530 °C for 12 h and fractured at -40 °C. The second image shows the fracture surface of a 1.56 wt.% Si specimen prepared under identical aging and fracture testing conditions. Both specimens exhibit dominant transgranular cleavage-like brittle fracture features, with distinct slip traces observed on favorably oriented cleavage facets for both samples. No dramatic difference in fracture morphology is visually distinguished between the two samples with different Si contents. The observed cleavage-like fracture characteristics verify the low-temperature brittle fracture mechanism of the aged high Mn-Al steel, which is related to glide plane decohesion induced by κ-carbide precipitation and planar slip, supporting the conclusion that higher Si addition reduces the low-temperature notch toughness of aged Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

These two scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images with a unified scale bar of 20 μm display Charpy V-notch (CVN) fracture surfaces of Fe-30Mn-9Al-2.7Si-0.9C lightweight high manganese aluminum steel under two processing conditions. The first subgraph (as-hot-rolled specimen) exhibits transgranular cleavage fracture morphology with visible {111} slip traces on cleavage facets, corresponding to brittle fracture with low CVN impact energy of ~22-23 J, which is caused by κ-carbide precipitation during slow cooling after hot rolling. The second subgraph (specimen subjected to hot rolling, 10 min solution treatment at 1100 °C and water quenching) shows completely ductile fracture morphology with dense dimples, corresponding to a significantly improved CVN impact energy of ~160 J. This figure confirms that proper solution treatment can eliminate harmful κ-carbide precipitation and short-range ordering formed after hot rolling, thus greatly enhancing the impact toughness of this alloy system.

This figure presents four scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of fracture surfaces from Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steel dynamic fracture toughness (DFT) specimens, all aged at 530 °C, with a 50 μm scale bar provided for each subplot. Subplot (a) corresponds to 1.07 wt% Si content and 300 BHN hardness, (b) to 1.56 wt% Si and 300 BHN, (c) to 1.07 wt% Si and 350 BHN, and (d) to 1.56 wt% Si and 350 BHN. Morphology comparison shows that lower Si content and lower aging hardness produce ductile fracture features dominated by dimples. As Si content and aging hardness increase, the fracture mode transitions to brittle transgranular cleavage. Subplots (c) and (d) exhibit visible {111} slip traces on oriented cleavage facets, indicating fracture occurs via glide plane decohesion. These results support that increased Si addition and higher age hardening reduce the dynamic fracture toughness of the studied steel, raising brittle fracture propensity.

This group of two bar charts characterizes inclusion features in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.9C lightweight austenitic steels with three different Si contents: 0.59 wt%, 1.07 wt%, and 1.56 wt%. The first subplot presents the number density of different inclusion types including AlMnSO, AlN, Al-N-Mn-O, Al-N-Mn-S, Mn-Al-O, MnO, MnS, other oxides, other oxi-sulfides, other sulfides, and micro-defects/porosity, with unit particles/mm². It shows that the number density of most inclusion types is comparable among the three steels, except for porosity: the 0.59 wt% Si steel has a much higher porosity density of ~80 particles/mm², more than twice that of 1.07 wt% Si steel and nearly double that of 1.56 wt% Si steel. The second subplot shows the average size of each inclusion type in microns, indicating that the average size of the same type of inclusion is largely similar across the three Si content groups, with MnO inclusions reaching the maximum value of ~3.6 μm in 1.07 wt% Si steel. This figure supports the conclusion that the lower toughness of aged 0.59 wt% Si steel is attributed to its higher porosity content, and Si content has no significant effect on the number density and size of other inclusions except porosity.
